coredata.dc:description: Doping is an effective approach to regulate the physicochemical properties of carbon dots (CDs) and has received increasing attention in recent years. After CDs are doped with heteroatoms, their electronic structure, nanostructure, and chemical composition will be changed due to the overlapping atomic orbitals of the heteroatoms and carbon atoms, as well as the push/pull electron effect of heteroatoms. As compared with the non-metallic atoms, metal ions have more electron and unoccupied orbitals, and larger atomic radius, doping CDs with metal ions may induce noticeable alterations of the optical, electronic, and magnetic properties by changing the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs. In this review, we summarize the synthesis methods of metal ions-doped CDs (M−CDs), discuss the novel physicochemical properties originating from the doping of metal ions, and provide examples of applications in sensing, imaging, phototherapy, optoelectronics, and catalysis. Furthermore, we describe challenges in preparing M−CDs and provide an outlook for their future development.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping is an effective approach to regulate the physicochemical properties of carbon dots (CDs) and has received increasing attention in recent years. After CDs are doped with heteroatoms, their electronic structure, nanostructure, and chemical composition will be changed due to the overlapping atomic orbitals of the heteroatoms and carbon atoms, as well as the push/pull electron effect of heteroatoms. As compared with the non-metallic atoms, metal ions have more electron and unoccupied orbitals, and larger atomic radius, doping CDs with metal ions may induce noticeable alterations of the optical, electronic, and magnetic properties by changing the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs. In this review, we summarize the synthesis methods of metal ions-doped CDs (M−CDs), discuss the novel physicochemical properties originating from the doping of metal ions, and provide examples of applications in sensing, imaging, phototherapy, optoelectronics, and catalysis. Furthermore, we describe challenges in preparing M−CDs and provide an outlook for their future development.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were accidentally discovered by Scrivens et al. in 2004 during the electrophoretic fractionation of carbon nanotubes [1]. Compared to organic dyes or traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CDs have the advantages of easy preparation and surface modification, good photostability and water dispersibility, as well as tunable fluorescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [2-5]. In generally, the core of CDs consists of crystalline sp2 carbon, whereas the surface is rich in carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups [6-8]. This unique nanostructure endows CDs with special properties, enabling their extensive use in fluorescence sensing [9-11], imaging, phototherapy [12,13], optoelectronic devices, and catalysis [14,15].

Doping has been widely used in the research of semiconductor materials since the optical, electrical, and magnetic properties of semiconductors can be modified to the desired extent [16]. Due to the overlap of the atomic orbitals of the heteroatoms and the carbon atoms, as well as the push/pull electron effect of the heteroatoms, introducing heteroatoms into the CDs nanostructure may induce a significant alteration in their electronic structure, nanostructure, and chemical composition [17]. Metal ions have more electrons and unoccupied orbitals and larger atomic radius than the non-metallic atoms. After the CDs are doped with metal ions, their electron density distribution and energy gap undergo drastic changes, enabling the adjustment of the CDs’ physical and chemical properties [18,19].

Although the preparation and applications of heteroatom-doped CDs have been summarized in various review articles [20-22], only two reviews are focused on M−CDs. For example, in 2018, Lin et al. described in detail the synthesis methods, physicochemical characteristics, and applications of M−CDs in the environmental, biological, and energy related fields [23]. In the other review, Zhai et al. summarized the advances of rare-earth-ion-doped CDs (RE-CDs), including fabrication strategies and applications in sensing and bioimaging [24]. However, research on M−CDs has developed rapidly, and many synthesis strategies and applications have been reported recently. Therefore, there is a need for reviewing the advanced materials and state-of-the-art techniques for designing M−CDs. Furthermore, the relationship between the unique physicochemical properties of M−CDs and metal ion-doping should be analyzed in-depth.

In this review, we summarize and discuss the synthesis methods and applications of M−CDs reported at present, especially in the last five years. The effect of metal ions doping on the properties of CDs, including optical, chemical, biological, magnetic, and electrical properties, were fully discussed. The applications of M−CDs were classified and summarized in detail, including optical sensors (including fluorescent and colorimetric sensors), optoelectronic devices, imaging (including fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, and photoacoustic imaging), phototherapy, catalysis (including photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, chemical catalysis, and biocatalysis) (Fig. 1 ). More importantly, we also comprehensively summarize the challenges and obstacles encountered in the development of M−CDs, and propose perspectives for the further exploration of novel applications.

Generally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are also considered CDs. The preparation methods of CDs are usually divided into top-down and bottom-up approaches. The latter including microwave- and ultrasonic-assisted solvothermal, hydrothermal, and pyrolysis are most commonly used for preparing M−CDs [25].

The solvothermal method has been widely adopted for the synthesis of M−CDs due to its low cost and high efficiency. CDs doped with manganese (Mn) [26,27], copper (Cu) [28,29], magnesium (Mg) [30], zinc (Zn) [31], gadolinium (Gd) [32], cobalt (Co) [33], silver (Ag) [34], and chromium (Cr) [35] ions have been prepared by solvothermal treatment of carbon sources and metallic salts. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized Co-CDs by solvothermal treatment of 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) and CoCl2 in the presence of ethanol. The fluorescence of Co-CDs could be quenched by Cr4+ (Fig. 2 a) [33]. Zhou et al. used citric acid (CA) and urea as carbon sources, Mn(OAc)2 as a metal ions source, and toluene as a solvent to synthesize Mn-CDs with a high FLQY of 68.6% (Fig. 2b). The high FLQY of the Mn-CDs was originated from the Mn2+ increasing the conjugated sp2-domains and surface states. In contrast, in the absence of Mn(OAc)2, the obtained CDs showed a lower FLQY (46%) [27].

However, most organic solvents are toxic, expensive, and may cause serious damage to human health. In contrast, water is a green and cheap solvent, thus, the hydrothermal method has become increasingly popular for preparing M−CDs. In 2019, Cr-CDs were prepared by Jiang et al. via hydrothermal treatment of tris(2,4-pentanedionato) Cr(III) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) at 200 °C for 12 h [36]. The Cr-CDs aqueous solution showed blue-green fluorescence with a FLQY of 20%. Zhao et al. synthesized blue fluorescent Er-CDs by the hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 10 h using triethylene tetramine hexaacetic acid (C18H30N4O12) and Er2O3 powder as the precursors [37]. In the absence of Er2O3, the obtained CDs had a low FLQY. The high FLQY likely occurred because the Er3+ enhanced the electron transfer and inhibited the charge recombination of the CDs.

Moreover, ultrasound irradiation, which generates heat, has also been used to prepare M−CDs. For example, Bourlinos et al. used xylene, ethylbenzene, and FeCl3 as precursors to prepare Fe-CDs in a sonication bath for 24 h, as shown in Fig. 2c [38]. It should be noted that solvothermal and ultrasound-assisted solvothermal methods are generally time-consuming. In contrast, the microwave-assisted solvothermal method utilizes microwave radiation to generate high heat rapidly to dehydrate and carbonize the carbon source in a few minutes. This approach has been used to produce numerous types of M−CDs, such as Gd-CDs [39], Au-CDs [40], Eu-CDs [41], Fe-CDs [42], and Zn-CDs [43]. For example, Dong et al. used EuCl3 and PEG400 as precursors to prepare Eu-CDs within 3 min [41]. The obtained Eu-CDs aqueous solution exhibited bright red fluorescence under UV light irradiation. Interestingly, when the conventional hydrothermal method was used, the obtained Eu-CDs showed weak blue fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 2d. Yang et al. used glucosamine hydrochloride as the carbon source and Zn(OAc)2 as the metal ion source to prepare Zn-CDs at 130℃ within 60 min [43]. A Zn-free control sample (N-CDs) was also synthesized under similar experimental conditions. The FLQY of the N-CDs and N,Zn-CDs were 2.6% and 74%, respectively. The high FLQY of the Zn-CDs was attributed to the Zn2+ doping, which changed the ordered structure of the carbon ring, and the newly generated emission traps further improved the electron-hole removal.

Pyrolysis is another commonly used method for synthesizing M−CDs [44]. Liu et al. prepared blue fluorescent Eu-CDs through the pyrolysis of CA and Eu(NO3)3 at 200 °C [45]. The maximum emission of the Eu-CDs solution occurred at 465 nm under 350 nm excitation, with a FLQY of 4.7%. Huang et al. reported the pioneering synthesis of blue fluorescent Tb-CDs through the pyrolysis of CA and Tb(NO3)3 at 190 °C for 30 min [46]. The obtained Tb-CDs showed good stability and oxidation resistance in a salt medium and a high-concentration H2O2 solution. Bera et al. produced Ru-CDs by pyrolyzing a mixture of CA and RuCl3 that was subsequently heated in a heating bath under continuous stirring with a magnetic stirrer for 5 h at 250oC [47]. The FLQY of the CDs increased with an increase in the doping concentration of Ru.

The solvothermal method for preparing M−CDs is simple and has a wide range of carbon sources. In addition, the properties of M−CDs can be controlled by selecting parameters such as solvent type and pH value, reaction temperature and pressure. The temperature is usually below 200 ℃. In contrast, the preparation of M−CDs by pyrolysis method has certain requirements for carbon source, which requires organic molecules with low melting point as carbon source. In addition, it is usually carried out at high temperature. The prepared M−CDs have less surface functional groups and poor water solubility.

M−CDs−based optical sensors have been utilized due to their excellent photostability and rich binding sites [48]. The sensing mechanisms primarily include photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), charge transfer, and the inner filter effect (IFE) [49]. We categorize M−CDs based optical sensors as fluorescent and colorimetric sensors in this review.

Liu et al. prepared germanium-doped CDs (Ge-CDs) using CA and Ge-132 as precursors via the pyrolysis method [44]. The obtained Ge-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence with a FLQY of 8.9%, whereas the CDs synthesized by the same method without Ge-doping had a lower FLQY of 5.6%. The high FLQY of the Ge-CDs results in excellent sensitivity for sensing applications. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the added Hg2+ leads to the aggregation of Ge-CDs due to the strong coordination interactions of Hg2+ with –OH and –COOH on the surface of the Ge-CDs, thus quenching their fluorescence. Zhang et al. prepared blue fluorescent La-CDs via the solvothermal treatment of adenosine disodium triphosphate (ATP) and LaCl3 [50]. The obtained La-CDs showed good stability under high-salt condition. After the addition of Hg2+, the strong chelation of Hg2+ with –COOH, -PO4 3−, and the –NH2 groups led to the fluorescence quenching of the La-CDs through the charge transfer mechanism. The La-CDs showed good selectivity and sensitivity toward Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.1 μM. He et al. prepared Fe-CDs-Au NC nanoprobes at two emission wavelengths (445 and 650 nm) for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Zn2+ [51]. In that study, the blue fluorescence of the Fe-CDs was independent of Zn2+, whereas the red fluorescence of the Au NCs increased with an increasing Zn2+ concentration, which was attributed to the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) enhancement of the Au NCs. The fluorescent color of the mixture solution changed from blue to pink after adding Zn2+.

Besides metal ions, M−CDs were also used for biomolecule detection. Hu et al. prepared blue fluorescent Gd-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of tryptophan, GdCl3, and glucose [52]. As shown in Fig. 3b, due to the strong π-π stacking interaction between the CMPs@GOx and the Gd-CDs, the fluorescence of the Gd-CDs was quenched by the carbon microparticle@glucose oxidase (CMP-GOx) complex through the FRET mechanism. However, glucose can enter the space between the CMP@GOx and Gd-CDs, causing the release of Gd-CDs, thus disturbing the FRET process from the Gd-CDs to the GOx@CMPs and restoring the fluorescence of the Gd-CDs.

The metal ions in the M−CDs also act as coordinate sites for binding with analytes. Li et al. prepared blue-green fluorescent Cr-CDs with a FLQY of 20% via hydrothermal treatment of tris(2,4-pentanedionato) Cr(III) and PEI. The obtained Cr-CDs were suitable for detecting p-Nitrophenol (p-NP) [36]. According to Fig. 3c, the addition of p-NP led to the quenching of the blue-green fluorescence of the Cr-CDs, whereas the fluorescence lifetime remained unchanged. Since the absorption spectrum of p-NP overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the Cr-CDs, the fluorescence quenching was assumed to be the static quenching mechanism and the IFE. Recently, Liu et al. synthesized Cu-CDs by a solvothermal method using folic acid and CuCl2 as the precursors and ethanol as the solvent [53]. The obtained Cu-CDs exhibited dual fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 470 nm, the first peak was ascribed to the changes in the structural and surface state of the CDs after Cu doping. The Cu-CDs exhibited a good fluorescence quenching response towards ascorbic acid (AA) from 0.02 to 40 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 17.8 nM (Fig. 3d). Furthermore, the quenching mechanism was attributed to the formation of a new non-fluorescent complex by a chelation interaction between Cu2+ in the CDs and –OH in the AA.

Colorimetric sensors are promising for detecting toxic pollutants because the results can be visually interpreted [54]. Zhuo et al. prepared Mn-CDs with an oxidase-like activity and capable of catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into oxTMB, changing the solution color from colorless to blue, with an absorption peak at 652 nm [55]. Interestingly, the addition of AA resulted in the transformation back to a colorless solution through a redox reaction. The obtained Mn-CDs showed excellent sensitivity to AA with an LOD of 9 nM. Based on similar principles, Zhao et al. obtained a colorimetric sensor based on Mo-CDs that possessed a peroxidase-like activity for cholesterol detection [56]. As shown in Fig. 3e, the Mo-CDs easily oxidated TMB to oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, and the solution color was changed from colorless to blue. In addition, by integrating the cholesterol oxidase (Chox)-triggered oxidation of cholesterol, the Mo-CDs-based cascade colorimetric biosensor could be applied for cholesterol detection. This result demonstrated the ability of a Mo-CDs catalytic system with high sensitivity for H2O2 for monitoring an enzyme-triggered reaction, where H2O2 was one of the catalysis products. The Mo-CDs-based biosensor for cholesterol detection exhibited outstanding selectivity and high sensitivity with an LOD of 7 µM. The excellent sensing performance was attributed to the Mo doping, which facilitated the electron transfer and enhanced the catalytic activity of the CDs.

Chen et al. reported the synthesis of CDs@5′-adenosine monophosphate (AMP)/Eu nanocomposites, which exhibited two emission peaks at 430 and 615 nm at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm [57]. The fluorescence of Eu3+ was enhanced by adding oxytetracycline (OTC), and the fluorescence of the CDs was quenched by OTC through the IFE since the absorption spectrum of OTC overlaps with both the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The solution color changed from blue to pink, as shown in Fig. 3f. Zhou et al. used ammonium iron(III) citrate and urea as precursors to prepare Fe-CDs via a one-step hydrothermal treatment. The obtained Fe-CDs were promising fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensors for Fe3+ detection [58]. As shown in Fig. 3g and 3 h, the blue fluorescence of the Fe-CDs was quenched, and the absorbance band at 335 nm increased with the addition of Fe3+. It suggested that the effective coordination or chelation interactions between Fe3+ and various functional groups on the surface of the Fe-CDs induced alterations in the conjugate structure.
Here we summarize recently reported M−CDs−based sensors in Table 1 .

Light-emitting diode (LED) is an ideal light source due to its high efficiency, long lifetime, and low power consumption [67-69]. QDs have been considered as a potential replacement for LEDs, however, their complex preparation process and the need for toxic heavy metal ions have restricted their large-scale application [70]. Since CDs possess fluorescence tunability, high photostability, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and high electron mobility, they are highly suitable for LEDs [71]. Researchers focused on M−CDs with enhanced carrier recombination in the trap states on the CDs surface to improve the LED performance, ensure a high FLQY, and the ability to emit solid-state light in contrast to pure CDs.

Cheng et al. used ZnCl2 and CA as precursors to prepare yellow fluorescent Zn-CDs with a high FLQY of 51.2% [72]. The high FLQY of the Zn-CDs was attributed to the rapid radiative recombination of the electrons and holes trapped on the Zn-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the Zn-CDs were successfully embedded into a white LED (WLED). Furthermore, the Zn-CDs solution was used to create fluorescent patterns using inkjet printing. As expected, the patterns exhibited bright yellow fluorescence. Zhang et al. prepared high-quality yellow fluorescent Mn-CDs using toluene as the solvent, CA and urea as the carbon sources, and Mn(OAc)2 as the metal ion source [27]. The FLQYs of the CDs and Mn-CDs were 46% and 68.8%, respectively. In that study, the Mn2+ was assumed to attract the CA through intermolecular coordination bonding, resulting in a large molecular structure with many surface groups. These ions then reacted with urea and were ultimately converted into Mn-CDs with large conjugated sp2-domains and enriched with the surface groups. The LED chip was fabricated using the yellow emissive Mn-CDs that emitted a warm light with a correlated color temperature of 1596 K (Fig. 4b). The effective synthesis strategy of solvent tuning and metal ions enhancement enables the development of high-efficiency M−CDs with multiple properties for LED applications.

The imaging applications of M−CDs can be categorized as fluorescence imaging (FLI), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and photoacoustic imaging (PAI) The specific applications are described in detail below.

FLI has become a powerful technique for clinical diagnosis because of its high sensitivity, non-invasiveness, and real-time monitoring. CDs have been considered promising candidates in the field of FLI due to their outstanding fluorescence stability, the absence of fluorescence blinking, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, adjustable excitation wavelength, and tunable emission wavelength [73]. More importantly, M−CDs have been employed as imaging and labeling agents for tracking molecular targets in living cells, tissues, and other biological systems [30,74].

Sahu et al. synthesized orange fluorescent Mn-CDs with excellent cell penetration ability and low toxicity, providing opportunities for intracellular and cells FLI [75]. The synthesized CDs without Mn2+ exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 600 nm (λex = 460 nm) with FLQY of 23.1%. After doping with Mn2+, its FLQY was increased to 28.5%. Mn-CDs also showed good fluorescence stability after storing six days. Moreover, MTT results demonstrated that the Mn-CDs have excellent biocompatibility and are an ideal bioimaging reagent.

Due to the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence of Mn-CDs, the Mn-CDs incubated cells or bacterials show strong green and red fluorescence under 420–460 nm and 520–550 nm wavelengths light excitation, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Sun et al. synthesized Mn-CDs using CA and Mn tetraphenyl porphyrin (MnTPP) as precursors [76]. The MTT results demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of the Mn-CDs, with a HeLa cell survival rate above 85%. Due to the excitation-dependent emission properties, the Mn-CDs were shown to be suitable as a multi-color bioimaging agent. As shown in Fig. 5b, the HeLa cells and zebrafish treated with the Mn-CDs showed blue, green, and red fluorescence under excitation of different light wavelengths.

Luo et al. synthesized Sn@S-CDs by the cross-linked interaction of SnCl4 and sulfonated groups (-SO3 −) on the surface of sulfonated carbon dots (S-CDs) [77]. After complexing with Sn4+, the size of the obtained Sn@S-CDs reduced to 1.8 nm, and the FLQY increased to 32%, and the QY of singlet oxygen (1O2) of Sn@S-CDs was as high as 37% (the FLQY of S-CDs was 27%). In addition, Sn@S-CDs showed significant biocompatibility, and different concentrations of unirradiated Sn@S-CDs have little effect on the overall viability of cells.

MRI is a non-invasive imaging technology that produces three-dimensional detailed anatomical images. Gd3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, and Ni2+ have been widely used in MRI due to their high r 1 relaxivity values and inherent physicochemical properties [78-80]. He et al. prepared Gd-CDs from ptaea, CA, and Gd-complex (Gd-DOTA) and exhibited 445 nm emission at 360 nm excitation [81]. As shown in Fig. 5c, Gd-CDs have a brighter signal with r1 values of 57.63 mM−1 s−1. Because of the high positive charge on the surface of the Gd-CDs, leading to easy phagocytic clearance due to the electrostatic interaction and the relatively small particle size, which is conducive to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. In addition, the Gd-CDs exhibited promising potential to serve as nano-theranostic agents for MR/FL dual-model imaging and gene therapy

Murugan et al. used lemon juice as the carbon source to obtain different ethanediamine (EDA)-functionalized CDs (TMCDs) which were doped with different metal ions (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, and Ni2+) by one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis at 800 W within 6 min (Fig. 5d) [82]. These TMCDs showed excellent biocompatibility and could be easily uptaken by SW480 cells. Bright green fluorescent signals were observed from the TMCDs incubated cells and zebrafish. Moreover, in vivo MRI was also demonstrated by using zebrafish as the model. Zhao et al. prepared Fe-CDs as a biocompatible nanoprobe for MRI application by hydrothermal treatment of glutathione (GSH), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and FeSO4 [83]. The MRI images of the mice were collected after 10 min intravenous injection of Fe-CDs aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 5e, an enhanced positive contrast MR signal in the brain, liver, and especially in the kidney were observed, suggesting that the CDs maybe eliminated through the renal routes due to their ultrasmall size.

Gu et al. prepared Mn-CDs by microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of CA, urea, and MnCl2 [84]. These Mn-CDs had a high r1 value of 6.23 mM−1∙s−1 and good cytocompatibility, indicating the CDs’ feasibility as MRI agents for small brain glioma imaging. As shown in Fig. 5f, a pronounced MR signal was observed in the small brain glioma region within 30 min after injecting the Mn-CDs. The response lasted 2 h, confirming the ability of the Mn-CDs to enter the brain and accumulate in the glioma region.

Du et al. synthesized gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method designing for MRI [85]. After tail vein injection of Gd-CDs, they showed a relatively long circulation time (∼6h), achieving efficient passive tumor targeting. Gd-CDs accumulate in the kidneys and can clear the bladder from the body. Importantly, the Gd-CDs exhibited good biocompatibility, with excellent performance in longitudinal relaxation rate (r 1) of 6.45 mM−1 s−1 and radiosensitization (Fig. 5g). These results indicated that Gd-CDs was an excellent T 1 contrast agent and radiosensitizer.

As compared with metal complexes, M−CDs show relatively low cytotoxicity, which can be attributed to low metal ions excretion came from the inertness of the carbon cage. Therefore, M−CDs, causing less toxic to the human body, have great potential in disease diagnosis as MRI contrast agents with high-efficiency metabolic functions. More importantly, among various M−CDs, Gd-CDs can act as radiosensitizers due to its doping of radioactive ions, which can make it potential for the radiotherapy of solid tumors.

PAI is an advanced imaging technology based on ultrasonic signal generation by the photothermal expansion of light-absorbing tissue under pulsed laser irradiation. This method combines the advantages of optical imaging and ultrasound imaging, such as high contrast, high spatial resolution, deep tissue penetration, and low health risk. At present, various types of PAI agents, including small molecule dyes, organic and inorganic nanoparticles, were developed for imaging cancer and cardiovascular diseases [86].

Metal ions doping leads to a red-shift in the absorption spectrum of the CDs to the near-infrared (NIR) region, which is suitable for PAI. As shown in Fig. 5h, Wu et al. used o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and L-Cys as precursors to prepared CDs by using the hydrothermal method. The CDs was further mixed with Cu2+ aqueous solution without stirring for 2 h, and then surface modified with HS-PEG and HS-PEG-RITC. The obtained Cu-CD NSs aqeous solution showed brigh red fluorescence originated from RITC, strong absorbance in the NIR region, and high photothermal conversion efficiency of 41.3% [87]. The applications of Cu-CD NSs in FLI, PAI, and photothermal (PT) imaging guided photothermal therapy (PTT) were demonstrated.
Here we summarized the use of M−CDs for imaging applications in Table 2 .

Phototherapy, including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and PTT, is a non-invasive cancer treatment in which phototherapy agents are used to convert light energy into reactive oxygen species (OH, O2 ·−, 1O2) or heat, leading to local death of cancer cells [95,96]. M−CDs have been used as phototherapy agents due to their unique optical properties, high water solubility, and photostability [97,98]. Bi et al. synthesized green fluorescent Cu-CDs by a pyrolytic technique using acrylic acid and Cu(NO3)2 as precursors, (NH4)2S2O8 as the initiator, and hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent (Fig. 6 a) [99]. The obtained Cu-CDs had a high FLQY of 24.4% and 1O2 quantum yield of 36%, and thus could be used to FLI-guided PDT of cancer.

Effective approaches have been developed for in situ generation of oxygen based on the acidic pH and high H2O2 concentration in tumor to overcome hypoxia and further enhance the PDT efficiency [100,101]. Wang et al. prepared Mn-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of manganese(II) phthalocyanine (Mn-Pc), and ethyl alcohol as the solvent and then assembled Mn-CDs with DSPE-PEG to improve the water solubility and biocompatibility of Mn-CDs (Fig. 6b) [102]. The obtained Mn-CDs assembly showed good physiological stability, NIR emission, high relaxivity, and excellent 1O2 generation ability, and thus FLI and MRI-guided PDT of cancer was achieved. More importantly, the Mn-CDs effectively catalyzed H2O2 to generate O2, mitigating tumor hypoxia and significanlty increasing the PDT efficiency.

In another study, Cu,N-CDs were synthesized as a new class of NIR light-triggered therapeutic agents by hydrothermal treatment of EDTA·2Na and CuCl2 (Fig. 6c) [103]. The NCuN coordination increased the absorbance of CDs in the NIR region and promoted the electron transfer during the photooxidation. Under NIR laser irradiation, the Cu,N-CDs could generate 1O2 and heat, thus acting as FLI and thermal imaging agent to enable the visualization of the in vitro and in vivo PDT treatment processes. Recently, Tian et al. developed nickel-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) exhibited strong NIR-II absorption which could be used in multimodal imaging (photothermal imaging (PTI), PAI, and MRI)-guided PTT (Fig. 6d) [104]. The Ni-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of EDTA, EDA, and NiCl3 at 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained Ni-CDs with intensive absorption in the NIR-II biowindow, prominent photothermal conversion efficiency of 76.1% and excellent photostability under NIR-II laser irradiation.

Numerous studies focused on developing advanced CDs as clean and sustainable energy conversion catalysts [105]. Compared to the general CDs, M−CDs provide new catalytic active centers, improve the catalytic activity, and increase the binding selectivity [106], which explains their wide use in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, chemical catalysis, and biocatalysis (Fig. 7 ).

Metal ions doping reduces the surface function of CDs, adjusting their bandgap and facilitating electron transfer between the reactions, endowing the M−CDs with unique catalytic properties and high performance [107,108]. Compared with traditional metal catalysts, M−CDs have the advantages of low cost, simple preparation, and easy access to raw materials, thus, M−CDs have become sought-after materials for catalysts [109]. Recently, numerous reports have been dedicated to the use of M−CDs for photocatalysis.

Wu et al. prepared Cu-CDs by one-step pyrolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] [110]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, Cu2+ were intercalated into the CDs through the chelation of Cu2+ and the carbon matrix. Cu2+ doping increased the abilities of CDs to accept and donate electrons by 2.5 and 1.5 times, respectively. Moreover, compared to the nonmetal-doped CDs, the photocatalytic efficiency of the Cu-CDs in the 1,4-dihydro-2,6-lutidine-3,5-dicarboxylate (1,4-DHP) photooxidation reaction was increased by 3.5 times. In another work, Mu et al. produced Cu,N-CDs by pyrolyzing Na2[Cu (EDTA)] and AA at 250 °C for 2 h and combining the mixture with Ag3PO4 to obtain Cu,N-CDs/Ag3PO4 nanomaterials that were used for neutral red (NR) degradation under visible light irradiation [111]. Since the N in the Cu,N-CDs has a higher electron affinity with Cu than that of C, the electron capture ability of the Cu,N-CDs is enhanced, improving the surface electron transport. In addition, the positively charged holes (h+) from the Ag-PO4 valence band reacts with H2O to generate free ·OH radicals, which participate in the NR degradation (see Fig. 8b).

In addition to the effective degradation of organic pollutants, M−CDs can also reduce the number of high-valence toxic metal ions through photocatalysis. For example, Khare et al. synthesized Zn-CDs using bougainvillea plant leaves and ZnO as precursors [112]. The experimentally assessed photoreduction activity of the Zn-CDs for the toxic Cr6 + revealed that the reduction rate was higher than under dark conditions (Fig. 8c). The improved photoreduction efficiency of the Zn-CDs was due to the relatively high catalytic activity of ZnO. Another reason for the high photocatalytic activity was the high adsorption rate, which significantly increased the effectiveness of the Cr6 + active sites in the Zn-CDs. Therefore, the electrons generated by the photocatalysis could easily contact the Cr6 + adsorbed on the surface of the Zn-CDs, stimulating the reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+.

CDs have been a potential candidate as electrocatalyst materials due to their advantages of low cost, high conductivity, easy modification, simple synthesis as well as rapid electron transfer and high surface area [113]. To be note, the metal ions doping to the CDs can further enhance the electrocatalytic properties greatly by introducing desirable active sites [114].

Yang et al. prepared Co,Fe co-doped CDs (Co,Fe-CDs) as an efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalyst by hydrothermal treatment of Co gluconate and ferrous gluconate salts, as shown in Fig. 8d [115]. The OER overpotential of the Fe,Co-CDs in an alkaline solution was 308 mV, which was lower than that of a complex Co/Fe-based catalyst. According to the density functional theory (DFT) calculation, grafting Co2+ active sites to the CDs surface is a promising approach to improve the charge transfer ability of CDs. Moreover, Fe3+ doping leads to alterations in the electronic structure of Co2+ and enhances the active center stability, thus improving the OER performance. In addition, ruthenium-doped CDs (Ru-CQDs) were used as electrocatalysts in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to the ability to promote intermolecular electron transfer. Li et al. synthesized CQDs by hydrothermal carbonization of plant leaves. The obtained CQDs was mixed with RuCl3 and then sequentially treated by hydrothermal and pyrolysis to prepare the Ru@CQDs (Fig. 8e) [116]. The fabricated Ru@CQDs exhibited excellent catalytic ability for the HER with an onset overpotential of 0 mV, and remarkable stability after 10,000 cyclic voltammetry cycles. Besides, the HER catalytic properties of the M−CDs were found to exceed those of Pt. In the same study, Ru@SC-CDs were produced by pyrolysis of self-crosslinking SC-CDs and RuCl3, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity and stability within the entire pH range (Fig. 8f) [117]. The DFT simulations revealed the synergistic effect between Ru and SC-CDs, which explained the outstanding catalytic activity and stability of the obtained CDs. In addition, Ru@SC-CDs exhibited high catalytic performance at low temperatures, indicating the possibility of their use in industrial-scale water electrolysis.

M−CDs are widely used to improve the electron transport capability of chemical catalysts [118]. For example, Du et al. prepared Cu-CDs by hydrothermal carbonization of CuCl2·H2O and EDTA and used them for catalytic reduction of p-NP in the presence of NaBH4 [119]. The synergistic effect of Cu and graphitic carbon endowed the catalyst with high catalytic performance, as shown in Fig. 9 a. Furthermore, the IFE caused changes in the fluorescence of the Cu-CDs catalyst during the catalytic reduction process. The results demonstrated that the catalytic reduction of p-NP could be monitored by the fluorescence performance of the catalyst. In another work, Huang et al. synthesized a new type of Cu-CDs by thermolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and AA at 250 °C for the Huisgen 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition (CuAAC) reaction, as shown in Fig. 9b [120]. The obtained Cu-CDs achieved high-efficiency light-activated click chemistry without using Cu2+ or reducing agents. Compared with traditional catalysts, the zigzag-edged Cu-CDs ensured the stability and fast release of Cu+. The catalyzation of the CuAAC reaction was attributed to the UV-induced exciton cleavage. This mechanism ensured that the electrons escaped from the Cu-CDs, leaving holes to compete with Cu+ and causing the release of the latter from the Cu-CDs.

In 2007, Yan et al. firstly proposed the concept of a “nanoenzyme” [121]. Since then, many nanomaterials, such as MnO2, CeO2, noble metal nanoparticles, prussian blue, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotubes, were found to have the properties of natural enzymes, including peroxidase, catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase. Nanoenzymes possess outstanding catalytic efficiency, good stability, and the ability of large-scale preparation, which are desirable in biomedicine, the chemical industry, and for food, agriculture, and environmental technologies [122]. Carbon-based nanoenzymes exhibit high catalytic ability, a wide application range, and excellent substrate-binding selectivity, enabling their use in biosensing, disease treatment, and pollutant removal [123]. Furthermore, metal ions doping endows the M−CDs with new catalytic activity centers, improving their stability and selectivity characteristics [28].

Wei et al. prepared Fe,N-CDs by solvothermal synthesis using L-tartaric acid, urea, and FeCl3 as sources [42]. The obtained Fe,N-CDs exhibited the intrinsic peroxidase activity through the Fe-catalyzed oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 9c). More importantly, the Fe,N-CDs showed better catalytic stability than horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and their activity remained above 71% compared to HRP (<10%) within a temperature range of 20–60 °C. Similar to Fe,N-CDs, Cu,N-CDs also possess high HRP activity. Lin et al. used EDTA and Cu(NO3)2 as sources to synthesize Cu,N-CDs (Fig. 9d) [124]. After the Cu,N-CDs were added to H2O2 and the peroxidase substrate TMB, the color of the TMB solution was changed from colorless to blue, indicating the intrinsic peroxide-like catalytic activity of the Cu,N-CDs. The HPR activity of the Cu,N-CDs was higher for the following reasons: First, the sp2 hybrid carbon structure in the Cu,N-CDs endows the nanomaterials with a catalytic activity similar to that of peroxidase. Second, the presence of –COOH/-CO- groups on the surface of the Cu,N-CDs play an important role in the reduction of H2O2. Finally, the five valence electrons from the N atoms and the Cu+ state increase the catalytic activity by changing the spin density and charge distribution of the carbon atoms.

Recently, Bao et al. prepared platinum-doped CNPs (PtCNPs) by hydrothermal treatment of K2PtCl4 and p-phenylenediamine (PPD). Their intrinsic peroxidase-like activity enabled one to catalyze TMB rapidly in the presence of H2O2, as shown in Fig. 9e [125]. The mechanism of PtCNPs-induced oxidation of the TMB was attributed to the donation of a lone pair of electrons from the amine groups to the PtCNPs, increasing the electron density and mobility of the PtCNPs and accelerating the electron transfer and TMB oxidation by H2O2. Therefore, based on the high HPR properties of the PtCNPs, a selective and sensitive colorimetric method was adopted for H2O2 and glucose detection.
Here we summarized the use of M−CDs for catalysis applications in Table 3 .

In this review, the recent advances in the synthesis and applications of M−CDs were presented. However, research on M−CDs is still in its early stage, thus, there are challenges that have to be researched and solved. (1) The doping principle of M−CDs is still poorly understood, and precision doping, such as doping at the edge or center of CDs is challenging. Moreover, the doping principle lacks structural characterization tools to determine whether the metal ions are in the lattice of the CDs or coupled to their surface. (2) It remains unclear how metal ions doping improves the properties of CDs, such as fluorescence wavelength, FLQY, and QY of 1O2. (3) Most of the currently reported M−CDs show absorption within the UV region, which are unsuitable for tissue penetration in imaging and treatment. (4) The true Stokes shift of the M−CDs is insufficient for the effective elimination of the biofluorescence background interference, making it difficult to achieve tunable fluorescence properties. (5) Metal ions have oxidation or reduction properties, which have great adverse impact on the formation of CDs during the synthesis process. (6) Metal ions may produce nano-sized metal oxides in the solvothermal process. Thus, it’s difficult to separate and distinguish M−CDs from the nano-sized metal oxides. Finally, even though M−CDs have excellent fluorescence performance, some metal ions are harmful to organisms when used in imaging applications and treatments. Based on the summary of the information from recent reports, we suggest the following future research prospects in the field of M−CDs.

(1) There is a need to conduct more in-depth investigations on the physical and chemical structure of M−CDs to improve the emission wavelength. (2) The development of M−CDs with excellent NIR emission performance enabling the effective penetration of tissues is a reliable route to meet the requirements for multi-functional applications for in vivo and in vitro bioimaging. (3) Signal responses, such as “turn on”, and the fluorescence ratio should be constructed for using M−CDs for sensing to improve the detection sensitivity and enable visual colorimetric detection. (4) The design and synthesis of M−CDs for specific cell targeting, diagnosis, and treatment applications is a relevant task. (5) M−CDs show better catalytic properties than CDs due to the more electrons and unoccupied orbitals of metal ions. (6) M−CDs doped with rare earth elements can realize the unique NIR emissions. (7) The abundant electronic energy levels of M−CDs make them have broad prospects in solar energy conversion, rechargeable hydride batteries, electronics, economic lighting fields, deserving further exploration in the future.

In brief, this review was aimed at analyzing the recent advances in M−CDs, including different synthesis strategies, physicochemical characteristics, and application fields. In particular, metal ions doping was shown to be a promising method to improve the optical, electrical, chemical, and magnetic properties of CDs by adjusting their electronic structure. Therefore, the outstanding performance of M−CDs for nanoprobing, optoelectronics, catalysis, imaging, and disease treatment applications was discussed in detail. We believe that through continuous efforts, M−CDs will soon play a more important role in our lives.
